content.text: Cloudflare Tunnel - Zero-Trust Homelab Ingress

A **production-grade ingress layer** for a self-hosted homelab server вҖ” exposing 12+ services publicly across multiple domains with **zero open ports**, zero public IP exposure, automatic TLS, and DDoS protection. All powered by a single Cloudflare Tunnel running as a Docker container.

This is the backbone of the entire homelab infrastructure. Every public-facing service вҖ” from the Kubernetes API to personal projects to early-stage business ideas вҖ” is routed through a single `cloudflared` daemon that creates an outbound-only encrypted tunnel to Cloudflare's edge network.

content.title: Average Latency
content.text: **~350ms average latency** for self-hosted APIs вҖ” competitive with budget VPS providers, achieved from a home server behind a residential ISP with no port forwarding. Cloudflare's global edge network handles the heavy lifting.
The Problem

Running a homelab server and exposing services publicly is harder than it sounds. The conventional approach вҖ” port forwarding on the home router вҖ” comes with a stack of real problems that make it unsuitable for anything beyond casual tinkering.

items: **No static IP** - Most residential ISPs assign dynamic IPs. Your DNS records go stale every time the IP rotates, taking services offline.
**Router port forwarding** - Exposes your home network's public IP. Every open port is a potential attack surface вҖ” bots scan the entire IPv4 space in under an hour.
**No TLS by default** - Setting up SSL certificates manually (Let's Encrypt + certbot) per service adds complexity and maintenance overhead.
**ISP restrictions** - Many residential ISPs block inbound traffic on ports 80 and 443 entirely, making standard web hosting impossible.
**DDoS exposure** - A direct connection to your home IP means a DDoS attack hits your home router вҖ” taking down your entire network, not just the targeted service.
**No access control at edge** - Without a CDN in front, there's no way to rate-limit, geo-block, or apply WAF rules before traffic reaches your server.

content.title: The Core Problem
content.text: Traditional homelab exposure reveals your home IP, requires open inbound ports, provides no TLS automation, and leaves you fully exposed to the internet. For a server running passion projects and early business ideas, this is unacceptable.
Solution - Cloudflare Tunnel

**Cloudflare Tunnel** (`cloudflared`) flips the connection model entirely. Instead of Cloudflare connecting inbound to your server, your server connects outbound to Cloudflare. The tunnel is a persistent encrypted connection вҖ” Cloudflare's edge receives public traffic and forwards it through the tunnel to your server. Your home IP is never revealed.

items: **Zero open ports** - No inbound ports on the router. The tunnel is outbound-only (QUIC/HTTP2 to Cloudflare).
**No public IP exposure** - Cloudflare acts as the public face. Your home IP is completely hidden from clients.
**Automatic TLS** - Cloudflare terminates TLS at the edge. Every subdomain gets HTTPS automatically вҖ” no certbot, no renewal cron jobs.
**DDoS protection** - Cloudflare absorbs DDoS attacks at the edge. Your server only receives clean, proxied traffic.
**Dynamic IP resilient** - The tunnel reconnects automatically when your ISP rotates your IP. DNS never needs updating.
**Multi-domain, multi-service** - One tunnel handles all 12 services across `vpjoshi.in`, `unetify.in`, and `joshistudio.com`.
**Free tier** - The entire setup runs on Cloudflare's free plan. No cost for tunnels, TLS, or DDoS protection.

content.title: One Tunnel, Three Domains
content.text: A single `cloudflared` instance routes traffic for **3 different domains** вҖ” `vpjoshi.in` (personal homelab), `unetify.in` (AI agent platform), and `joshistudio.com` (web agency). One process, one config file, zero extra cost.
System Architecture

content.title: Two Routing Paths
content.text: Services are reached via two paths вҖ” **direct port routing** (Grafana, n8n, PocketBase etc. on fixed ports) and **Traefik ingress** (stats, s3-drive, portfolio, unetify вҖ” all behind port 80, routed by Traefik inside k3s). The K8s API gets special treatment with `noTLSVerify: true` since it uses a self-signed cert.
Full K8s Ingress Flow
For services running inside the k3s cluster, requests travel through four layers before hitting the application pod вҖ” Cloudflare edge вҶ’ tunnel вҶ’ Traefik ingress вҶ’ pod.

content.code:
Client: GET https://stats.vpjoshi.in/api/metrics/stats
        в”Ӯ
        в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

в”Ӯ  - TLS termination              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  - DNS: stats.vpjoshi.in        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    вҶ’ CNAME to tunnel endpoint   в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ  Next.js 15, port 3000          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  namespace: apps-prod           в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

# Traefik IngressRoute for stats app
apiVersion: traefik.io/v1alpha1
kind: IngressRoute
metadata:
  name: stats-ingress
  namespace: apps-prod
spec:
  entryPoints:
    - web
  routes:
    - match: Host(`stats.vpjoshi.in`)
      kind: Rule
      services:
        - name: stats-svc
          port: 3000

content.title: Why Traefik over Nginx Ingress?
content.text: k3s ships with **Traefik as the default ingress controller** вҖ” no extra installation needed. It supports dynamic config via CRDs (`IngressRoute`), has a built-in dashboard, and handles routing for all k3s-hosted services with zero additional overhead on an 8GB RAM server.
K8s API Special Routing

The K8s API server (`k8s-api.vpjoshi.in`) gets special treatment in the tunnel config вҖ” it routes to port 6443 (HTTPS) with `noTLSVerify: true` because k3s uses a self-signed certificate. This allows `kubectl` and GitHub Actions to reach the cluster API remotely without VPN.

content.code:
# Tunnel config for K8s API
- hostname: k8s-api.vpjoshi.in
  service: https://192.168.x.x:6443
  originRequest:
    noTLSVerify: true   # k3s uses self-signed cert

# Access cluster from anywhere
export KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/homelab.yaml
kubectl get pods -n apps-prod

# Works from:
# - Developer laptop
# - GitHub Actions runner
# - Any machine with the kubeconfig
# No VPN, no SSH tunnel, no open firewall port

content.title: Security Note
content.text: `noTLSVerify: true` means the tunnel doesn't verify the k3s server certificate вҖ” but the connection from client to Cloudflare is still TLS-encrypted. Access is protected by the `ci-user` K8s service account token. For a homelab, this is an acceptable tradeoff. Production multi-team clusters should pass the CA cert explicitly.
Tunnel Configuration

The tunnel is configured via a single `config.yml` file. Each ingress rule maps a public hostname to an internal service вҖ” IP address and port. The final catch-all rule returns 404 for any hostname not explicitly listed.

content.code:
# config.yml
tunnel: <tunnel-id>
credentials-file: /home/nonroot/.cloudflared/<tunnel-id>.json

ingress:
  # Internal tools
  - hostname: filebrowser.vpjoshi.in
    service: http://192.168.x.x:1001

  - hostname: portainer.vpjoshi.in
    service: http://192.168.x.x:1002

  - hostname: ollama-api.vpjoshi.in
    service: http://192.168.x.x:1003

  - hostname: grafana.vpjoshi.in
    service: http://192.168.x.x:1005

  - hostname: n8n.vpjoshi.in
    service: http://192.168.x.x:1007

  # K8s API вҖ” HTTPS with self-signed cert
  - hostname: k8s-api.vpjoshi.in
    service: https://192.168.x.x:6443
    originRequest:
      noTLSVerify: true

  # Traefik ingress вҖ” port 80, routed internally by k3s
  - hostname: selfhosted-api.vpjoshi.in
    service: http://192.168.x.x:80

  - hostname: stats.vpjoshi.in
    service: http://192.168.x.x:80

  - hostname: s3-drive.vpjoshi.in
    service: http://192.168.x.x:80

  # unetify.in вҖ” AI agent platform
  - hostname: api.unetify.in
    service: http://192.168.x.x:80

  - hostname: pocketbase.unetify.in
    service: http://192.168.x.x:1008

  # joshistudio.com вҖ” web agency
  - hostname: pb.joshistudio.com
    service: http://192.168.x.x:1009

  # Catch-all
  - service: http_status:404

content.title: Port Strategy
content.text: Services on fixed ports (1001-1009) are Docker containers or standalone processes running directly on the host. Services on port 80 are routed through Traefik inside k3s вҖ” the tunnel sends all port-80 traffic to Traefik, which then routes by hostname to the correct pod.
Setup & Installation
1. Create the Tunnel

content.code:
# Install cloudflared
curl -L https://github.com/cloudflare/cloudflared/releases/latest/download/cloudflared-linux-amd64 \
  -o /usr/local/bin/cloudflared
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/cloudflared

# Authenticate with Cloudflare
cloudflared tunnel login
# вҶ’ opens browser, select your zone
# вҶ’ saves cert.pem to ~/.cloudflared/

# Create the tunnel
cloudflared tunnel create homelab
# вҶ’ generates tunnel ID and credentials JSON
# вҶ’ saves to ~/.cloudflared/<tunnel-id>.json

content.text: 2. Configure DNS

content.code:
# Create CNAME records for each hostname
# Points each subdomain to the tunnel endpoint
cloudflared tunnel route dns homelab stats.vpjoshi.in
cloudflared tunnel route dns homelab grafana.vpjoshi.in
cloudflared tunnel route dns homelab k8s-api.vpjoshi.in
# ... repeat for each hostname

# Each creates a CNAME:
# stats.vpjoshi.in вҶ’ <tunnel-id>.cfargotunnel.com

content.text: 3. Write config.yml

content.code:
mkdir -p ~/.cloudflared
# Write your config.yml as shown above
vim ~/.cloudflared/config.yml

content.text: 4. Run as Docker Container

content.code:
# Run cloudflared as a Docker container
docker run -d \
  --name cloudflared \
  --restart unless-stopped \
  --network host \
  -v ~/.cloudflared:/home/nonroot/.cloudflared:ro \
  cloudflare/cloudflared:latest \
  tunnel --config /home/nonroot/.cloudflared/config.yml run

# Verify it's connected
docker logs cloudflared
# вҶ’ INF Connection established connIndex=0
# вҶ’ INF Connection established connIndex=1
# вҶ’ INF Connection established connIndex=2
# вҶ’ INF Connection established connIndex=3
# (4 connections to Cloudflare edge вҖ” HA by default)

content.text: 5. Docker Compose (Recommended)

content.code:
# docker-compose.yml
version: '3.8'
services:
  cloudflared:
    image: cloudflare/cloudflared:latest
    container_name: cloudflared
    restart: unless-stopped
    network_mode: host
    volumes:
      - ~/.cloudflared:/home/nonroot/.cloudflared:ro
    command: tunnel --config /home/nonroot/.cloudflared/config.yml run

docker compose up -d cloudflared
docker compose logs -f cloudflared

content.title: network_mode: host
content.text: `network_mode: host` gives the cloudflared container direct access to the host network вҖ” it can reach services on `localhost` or `192.168.x.x` without any Docker networking complexity. Since the config maps to internal IPs, host networking is the simplest approach.
Full Services Map

content.code:
# vpjoshi.in вҖ” personal homelab
filebrowser.vpjoshi.in  вҶ’ Filebrowser  (file manager UI)
portainer.vpjoshi.in    вҶ’ Portainer    (Docker container manager)
ollama-api.vpjoshi.in   вҶ’ Ollama       (LLaMA 3.2 inference API)
grafana.vpjoshi.in      вҶ’ Grafana      (internal monitoring, auth protected)
k8s-api.vpjoshi.in      вҶ’ k3s API      (kubectl / GitHub Actions access)
n8n.vpjoshi.in          вҶ’ n8n          (workflow automation)
selfhosted-api.vpjoshi.in вҶ’ k3s Traefik (general API ingress)
stats.vpjoshi.in        вҶ’ Stats App    (public monitoring dashboard)
s3-drive.vpjoshi.in     вҶ’ S3 Drive     (self-hosted Google Drive)

# unetify.in вҖ” AI agent platform
api.unetify.in          вҶ’ Unetify API  (FastAPI backend, k3s)
pocketbase.unetify.in   вҶ’ PocketBase   (Unetify auth + realtime)

# joshistudio.com вҖ” web agency
pb.joshistudio.com      вҶ’ PocketBase   (Joshi Studio backend)

content.text: How Cloudflare Tunnel Works Internally

`cloudflared` establishes **4 persistent outbound connections** to Cloudflare's edge network вҖ” one per connection for redundancy. These use QUIC (UDP) by default, falling back to HTTP/2 (TCP). When a request arrives at Cloudflare's edge for a tunneled hostname, it's forwarded through one of these connections to `cloudflared`, which proxies it to the local service.

content.code:
# cloudflared maintains 4 connections to edge (HA)
cloudflared daemon
  в”Ӯ
  в”ңв”Җв”Җ conn 0 вҶ’ Cloudflare PoP (Mumbai)
  в”ңв”Җв”Җ conn 1 вҶ’ Cloudflare PoP (Chennai)
  в”ңв”Җв”Җ conn 2 вҶ’ Cloudflare PoP (Singapore)
  в””в”Җв”Җ conn 3 вҶ’ Cloudflare PoP (Mumbai)

# If one connection drops вҶ’ automatic reconnect
# If ISP IP changes вҶ’ tunnel reconnects, DNS unchanged
# Client never sees the reconnect

items: **QUIC protocol** вҖ” UDP-based, lower latency than TCP for most connections. Falls back to HTTP/2 if UDP is blocked.
**4 redundant connections** вҖ” if one edge PoP goes down, traffic automatically shifts to others.
**Automatic reconnect** вҖ” if the homelab loses internet briefly, `cloudflared` reconnects and all services come back without any DNS changes.
**Edge-side TLS** вҖ” Cloudflare terminates HTTPS at the edge. The tunnel itself is also encrypted. Two layers of encryption between client and server.
**No inbound firewall rules** вҖ” the home router needs zero configuration. All connections are outbound from the server.

content.text: Latency & Performance

Average API latency is **~350ms** вҖ” composed of Cloudflare edge routing (~20ms), tunnel overhead (~10ms), and server processing time (variable). For a self-hosted homelab on a residential ISP, this is competitive with budget VPS providers.

content.code:
# Latency breakdown (approximate)
Client вҶ’ Cloudflare edge:     ~20ms  (depends on client location)
Cloudflare вҶ’ tunnel:          ~10ms  (edge to homelab via tunnel)
Tunnel вҶ’ service/pod:         ~1ms   (local routing)
Server processing:            ~50-300ms (app dependent)
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
Total average:                ~350ms

# Comparison
Budget VPS (DigitalOcean BLR): ~80-150ms
Homelab via tunnel:             ~300-400ms
Managed cloud (AWS ap-south-1): ~50-100ms

content.title: Optimizing Latency
content.text: Latency is dominated by the residential ISP upload speed and Cloudflare PoP distance. For Indian users, Cloudflare routes through Mumbai or Chennai PoPs вҖ” keeping latency low. For static assets, Cloudflare's CDN caching eliminates the tunnel entirely вҖ” cached responses return in ~20ms.
Reliability & Uptime

`cloudflared` runs with `--restart unless-stopped` in Docker вҖ” it automatically restarts on crash or server reboot. With 4 redundant tunnel connections to different Cloudflare PoPs, a single edge node failure is fully transparent to users.

items: **Auto-restart** - Docker restart policy ensures cloudflared comes back after server reboots or crashes.
**4 redundant connections** - traffic shifts to healthy connections if one drops.
**ISP IP rotation resilient** - when the residential IP changes, cloudflared reconnects outbound. No DNS TTL propagation delay.
**Cloudflare uptime** - Cloudflare's edge has 99.99%+ uptime SLA. The homelab server is the only potential point of failure.
**Health monitoring** - Prometheus + Grafana monitors the homelab server. If the server goes down, Grafana alerts via the monitoring stack.

content.title: Single Point of Failure
content.text: The homelab is a single physical machine вҖ” if the server goes down, all services go down. The tunnel itself is redundant, but the hardware is not. For critical services, this is mitigated by keeping backups and having fast restore procedures. Passion projects and dev environments can tolerate occasional downtime.
Tech Stack

items: **cloudflared** - Cloudflare's open-source tunnel daemon (`cloudflare/cloudflared`).
**Docker** - cloudflared runs as a container with `network_mode: host`.
**Cloudflare DNS** - All subdomains are CNAME records pointing to the tunnel endpoint.
**Cloudflare TLS** - Automatic HTTPS for all hostnames. Edge certificates managed by Cloudflare.
**Traefik** - k3s built-in ingress controller. Routes port-80 traffic to the correct pod by hostname.
**k3s** - Lightweight Kubernetes. All app workloads run as K8s deployments behind Traefik.
**QUIC / HTTP2** - Transport protocols used by the tunnel connection to Cloudflare edge.

content.text: Useful Commands

content.code:
# Check tunnel status
docker logs cloudflared --tail 20

# List all tunnels in your account
cloudflared tunnel list

# Test a specific route
cloudflared tunnel ingress validate
cloudflared tunnel ingress rule https://stats.vpjoshi.in

# Check active connections
cloudflared tunnel info <tunnel-id>

# Restart tunnel after config change
docker compose restart cloudflared
